narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background and academic journey have been carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts performance on the bar exam to help ensure students succeed. The model assessed your Year 1 grades placing you in the 2nd decile at your school, your even lower 1st decile ranking in Year 3, a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 23.5, an undergraduate GPA of 2.6, and your plan to attend full-time. It calculated a 72.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to pass and thrive in the profession. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the bottom of your class signals potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework compared to peers who ranked much higher on average. Your LSAT score was the second strongest driver toward failure, well below the typical 36.7 average, suggesting the model sees it as a key hurdle for bar success that many admitted students exceed. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided the third biggest influence, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing since your 2.6 is closer to the dataset average of 3.21 than your other metrics. Overall, these top three factors—especially the sharp drop in your school deciles and low LSAT—stood out as mismatched with profiles of students who typically pass the bar, where stronger rankings and test scores dominate. While full-time status aligns with most successful applicants, it couldn't offset the primary concerns here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or heritage, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted improvement or additional coursework, and exploring programs with preparatory support for first-generation or Hispanic students like yourself. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors to law school success.
